name: mmc.exe
description: mmc.exe is the Microsoft Management Console, the host that runs the administrative snap-ins behind tools like Services, Event Viewer, and Device Manager. Administrators use it constantly. Attackers abuse it both as a signed proxy for execution and as a remote-execution path through its COM interface.
about:
mmc.exe loads management snap-ins, the modular consoles saved as `.msc` files, that administer parts of Windows. Opening Services, Event Viewer, or a custom `.msc` runs through mmc.exe. It also exposes a COM automation object, `MMC20.Application`, that can be driven locally or, over DCOM, remotely. The genuine binary lives at `C:\Windows\System32\mmc.exe`.

Legitimately, mmc is launched by administrators opening management consoles, so the process is ordinary on managed systems. The snap-in or `.msc` it loads, its parent, and whether it was driven through COM are what give an instance meaning.

anomaly_signals.signal: A `.msc` loaded from a user-writable path (`Temp`, `AppData`, `Downloads`)
mmc started as a DCOM object (`MMC20.Application`) by a remote request
Parent is an Office application, a script host, or an unfamiliar process

security_notes:
mmc.exe is a system-binary proxy (T1218.014). A crafted `.msc` can run a command or load a snap-in that executes attacker code under a trusted, signed Microsoft process, bypassing application-control rules that allow mmc. A `.msc` from a temp or download folder, or mmc spawning a shell, points to this.

mmc is also a lateral-movement path through DCOM. The `MMC20.Application` COM object can be instantiated on a remote machine and told to run a command, so the payload executes as a child of mmc.exe on the target rather than as a child of whatever reached in over the network. An mmc that spawns a shell with no interactive console open, especially one created via DCOM, is the case to investigate.

commands.usecase: Configure a snap-in to load a COM custom class (CLSID) that has been added to the registry
Modify HKCU\Environment key in Registry with COR profiler values then launch MMC to load the payload DLL.

sigma.title: HackTool - Potential Impacket Lateral Movement Activity
MMC Executing Files with Reversed Extensions Using RTLO Abuse
Potentially Suspicious Event Viewer Child Process
UAC Bypass via Windows Firewall Snap-In Hijack
Windows Default Domain GPO Modification via GPME
Potential Executable Run Itself As Sacrificial Process

triage.signal: A `.msc` loaded from a user-writable path (`Temp`, `AppData`, `Downloads`)
triage.signalHtml: A .msc loaded from a user-writable path (Temp, AppData, Downloads)
mmc spawning cmd.exe, powershell.exe, or other LOLBINs

triage.signal: mmc started as a DCOM object (`MMC20.Application`) by a remote request
triage.signalHtml: mmc started as a DCOM object (MMC20.Application) by a remote request

triage.signal: Parent is an Office application, a script host, or an unfamiliar process
triage.signalHtml: Parent is an Office application, a script host, or an unfamiliar process

about_html:
mmc.exe loads management snap-ins, the modular consoles saved as .msc files, that administer parts of Windows. Opening Services, Event Viewer, or a custom .msc runs through mmc.exe. It also exposes a COM automation object, MMC20.Application, that can be driven locally or, over DCOM, remotely. The genuine binary lives at C:\Windows\System32\mmc.exe.

Legitimately, mmc is launched by administrators opening management consoles, so the process is ordinary on managed systems. The snap-in or .msc it loads, its parent, and whether it was driven through COM are what give an instance meaning.
security_notes_html:
mmc.exe is a system-binary proxy (T1218.014). A crafted .msc can run a command or load a snap-in that executes attacker code under a trusted, signed Microsoft process, bypassing application-control rules that allow mmc. A .msc from a temp or download folder, or mmc spawning a shell, points to this.

mmc is also a lateral-movement path through DCOM. The MMC20.Application COM object can be instantiated on a remote machine and told to run a command, so the payload executes as a child of mmc.exe on the target rather than as a child of whatever reached in over the network. An mmc that spawns a shell with no interactive console open, especially one created via DCOM, is the case to investigate.
